title: Arch Bridge
summary: Arch bridges transfer weight as horizontal thrust restrained by abutments and vertical loads on supports. Romans were the first to fully realize their potential; the world's largest is the Chaotianmen Bridge.

facts.text: Arch bridges work by transferring weight partially as horizontal thrust restrained by abutments and partially as vertical loads on arch supports.
The possibly oldest surviving arch bridge is the Mycenaean Arkadiko Bridge in Greece, dating to about 1300 BC — still used by local inhabitants.
Roman arch bridges were usually semicircular, though some were segmental (less than a semicircle), which allowed greater flood water throughput.
The world's largest arch bridge is the Chaotianmen Bridge over the Yangtze River in Chongqing, China, with a span of 552 m (1,811 ft), opened April 29, 2009.
Scholar Vittorio Galliazzo's survey found 931 Roman bridges, mostly of stone, across as many as 26 countries.
The Solkan Bridge over the Soča River in Slovenia (completed 1905) is the longest railroad stone arch bridge, with a span of 220 metres.
Most modern arch bridges are made from reinforced concrete.

title: Golden Gate Bridge
summary: The Golden Gate Bridge is a suspension bridge spanning the Golden Gate strait in San Francisco. It opened May 27, 1937, and is recognized as one of the Wonders of the Modern World.

facts.text: The Golden Gate Bridge opened to the public on May 27, 1937.
Joseph Strauss served as chief engineer, with significant contributions from Leon Moisseiff, Irving Morrow, and Charles Ellis.
The bridge's main span is 4,200 feet (1,280 m); it held the record for longest suspension bridge main span until 1964.
The bridge's towers stand 746 feet (227 m) above water — the world's tallest on a suspension bridge until 1993 when surpassed by Mexico's Mezcala Bridge.
The bridge's color is officially 'international orange,' selected by consulting architect Irving Morrow to complement natural surroundings and enhance fog visibility.
Each main cable is made of 27,572 individual strands of wire; the total wire length used in both main cables is estimated at 80,000 miles.
The Golden Gate Bridge is recognized by the American Society of Civil Engineers as one of the Wonders of the Modern World.

title: Suspension Bridge
summary: A suspension bridge has its deck hung below suspension cables on vertical suspenders. The first modern examples were built in the early 19th century. Three forces act on any bridge: dead load, live load, and dynamic load.

facts.text: In a suspension bridge, the deck is hung below suspension cables on vertical suspenders called hangers.
The first modern suspension bridges were built in the early 19th century.
Three kinds of forces act on any bridge: dead load (the bridge's own weight), live load (traffic and weather), and dynamic load (earthquakes, sudden gusts).
Union Bridge (England/Scotland, 1820) was the oldest suspension bridge still carrying road traffic, with a 137 m main span.
Roebling's Delaware Aqueduct (USA, 1847) is the oldest wire suspension bridge still in service in the United States.
The suspension cables must be anchored at each end of the bridge since any load on the bridge is transformed into tension in the main cables.
The original Tacoma Narrows Bridge took only 19 months to construct; the Akashi Kaikyo Bridge took close to a decade.
